description:
Context:
    You are a security-operations log analyst who has just received a batch of certificate “drop-ins”.  
    Each certificate is accompanied by a one-line metadata file that contains its essential fields.  
    Your job is to triage the batch, identify which certificates are healthy, which are about to
    expire, and which are already expired, then produce two artefacts that a downstream monitoring
    system will ingest.

    Starting state of the machine:
    1. Directory /home/user/certs_incoming/ already exists and contains exactly three certificates
       and three accompanying metadata files:

       /home/user/certs_incoming/server_ok.crt  
       /home/user/certs_incoming/server_ok.meta  

       /home/user/certs_incoming/server_soon.crt  
       /home/user/certs_incoming/server_soon.meta  

       /home/user/certs_incoming/server_expired.crt  
       /home/user/certs_incoming/server_expired.meta  

       Every *.meta file is a single line, pipe-delimited, with the following fixed field order  
       subject|issuer|not_before|not_after  
       (the date fields are in ISO-8601 calendar form: YYYY-MM-DD)

       Example line (from one of the actual files):
       CN=api.example.com|CN=Example-CA|2023-06-01|2024-12-31

    2. No other files or directories are present in /home/user/cert_analysis/ (the directory itself
       is also not yet present – you will need to create it).

    Required end state:
    After you finish, the system must contain the following two new artefacts inside a freshly
    created directory /home/user/cert_analysis/ :

    A.  /home/user/cert_analysis/expiring_certs.csv  
        • CSV header must be exactly:  
          filename,subject,issuer,not_before,not_after,days_until_expiry,status  
        • One subsequent row per input certificate (three rows total).  
        • days_until_expiry is calculated as (not_after – today) where “today” means the calendar
          date at the moment your commands run, expressed as an integer number of days
          (rounding down; do not include time-of-day).  
        • status MUST be determined with this logic:  
          – “OK”              when days_until_expiry > 30  
          – “EXPIRING_SOON”   when 0 <= days_until_expiry <= 30  
          – “EXPIRED”         when days_until_expiry < 0  
        • The rows can be in any order, but each column must be comma-separated with no extra
          whitespace.  
        • Example of a syntactically valid file with made-up numbers (do NOT copy):  
          filename,subject,issuer,not_before,not_after,days_until_expiry,status  
          server_ok.crt,CN=api.example.com,CN=Example-CA,2023-06-01,2024-12-31,200,OK

    B.  /home/user/cert_analysis/analysis.log  
        • One log line per processed certificate (three lines total).  
        • Format MUST be:  
          YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS Processed <filename> - <status>  
          where the timestamp is the local system time at the moment the certificate was processed.  
        • Example (do NOT copy):  
          2024-06-01 14:36:07 Processed server_ok.crt - OK  
        • Lines can appear in any order as long as each certificate is logged exactly once.

    What you have to do (high-level):
    1. Create /home/user/cert_analysis/ with normal user permissions.  
    2. For every *.meta file in /home/user/certs_incoming/, read the metadata, calculate how many
       days remain until its not_after date, decide the correct status keyword, and build the CSV
       row.  
    3. Write the CSV file with the exact header and rows described above.  
    4. As you process each certificate, append the required line to analysis.log.  
    5. Double-check that both artefacts exist, are readable, and strictly follow the formats.

    Verification notes (read carefully – this is what the automated grader will look for):
    • expiring_certs.csv must exist at the exact path given, must be a plain text file, and must
      contain exactly four lines (one header + three data lines).  
    • The header must match character-for-character.  
    • For each certificate, the filename column must match the base filename of the *.crt file.  
    • status values must strictly be one of: OK, EXPIRING_SOON, EXPIRED (all caps, underscores).  
    • analysis.log must exist at the exact path, contain exactly three lines, each conforming to
      the timestamp + message format, with the same status string that appears in the CSV.  
    • The grader will parse both files and cross-check that every certificate present in
      /home/user/certs_incoming/ appears exactly once in both artefacts and that the status logic
      is correct relative to the system’s current date.

    Hints you may find useful (not mandatory):
    • You can parse the pipe-delimited *.meta files with cut, awk, or similar tools.  
    • To compute date arithmetic without external libraries, GNU date supports  
      `date -d "YYYY-MM-DD" +%s` to convert a calendar date to epoch seconds.  
    • Redirect (>>) to append to analysis.log instead of overwriting it.

    Deliverables to produce:
    1. /home/user/cert_analysis/expiring_certs.csv  
    2. /home/user/cert_analysis/analysis.log
truth:
Initial files that already exist before the agent starts:

    1. /home/user/certs_incoming/server_ok.crt               (PEM content, not used for grading)
    2. /home/user/certs_incoming/server_ok.meta
       Content (single line, no trailing newline):
       CN=api.example.com|CN=Example-CA|2023-06-01|2024-12-31

    3. /home/user/certs_incoming/server_soon.crt             (PEM content, not used for grading)
    4. /home/user/certs_incoming/server_soon.meta
       Content:
       CN=login.example.com|CN=Example-CA|2023-07-01|2024-07-01

    5. /home/user/certs_incoming/server_expired.crt          (PEM content, not used for grading)
    6. /home/user/certs_incoming/server_expired.meta
       Content:
       CN=legacy.example.com|CN=Legacy-CA|2022-05-01|2023-12-31

    The automated test will:
    a. Read today’s date from the system clock at grading time.  
    b. Parse the not_after values given above and compute days_until_expiry for each.  
       • For example, if the grader runs on 2024-06-15, the calculated days_until_expiry would be:  
         server_ok.crt       → 199 days   → status OK  
         server_soon.crt     →  16 days   → status EXPIRING_SOON  
         server_expired.crt  → -167 days  → status EXPIRED  
       The calculation will be dynamic, so the agent’s script must compute it on the fly.
    c. Confirm that the generated CSV and log files match the specification exactly, including
       field order, header spelling, and status logic.

    The grader will ignore the actual *.crt PEM payload; it only validates the *.meta data and the
    two artefacts the agent creates.

    Required permissions:
    • /home/user/cert_analysis/ must be user-writable (mkdir with default umask is fine).  
    • Files written inside must be readable by the grader (default permissions are acceptable).
